title: References
text:
References

The following references are cited across this portfolio, formatted in APA 7th edition style.

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
text:
Qualifier 2 · Inclusive Learning
My approach to teaching and supporting student learning is centred on clarity, accessibility, and active engagement — particularly in areas that students often perceive as difficult or unfamiliar, such as bioinformatics and computational data analysis. I recognise that many biology students experience anxiety when encountering quantitative or coding-based content, and I deliberately structure my teaching to reduce these barriers while maintaining academic rigour.
crumbs: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
section: Clear, Structured Explanations Linked to Biological Meaning
text:
Clear, Structured Explanations Linked to Biological Meaning
A key feature of my teaching is the use of clear, structured explanations supported by real-world examples. I aim to break down complex concepts into manageable components, while consistently linking each step back to the underlying biological question. This approach is particularly important in BIOL340, where students transition from experimental data generation to computational analysis.
During teaching sessions, I explicitly walk through analytical workflows, explaining not only what to do, but why each step is necessary and how it contributes to scientific interpretation. This reflects the principles of constructive alignment (Biggs & Tang, 2011), where teaching activities are explicitly designed to support students in achieving intended learning outcomes.
Student evaluation data consistently reflects the effectiveness of this approach. Across multiple subjects, I receive high scores for clarity of explanation, organisation, and subject knowledge (typically above 5.5/6.0), with particularly strong performance in bioinformatics teaching contexts. Students frequently comment that concepts are easy to understand and that the structured delivery helps them engage with complex material.
[Figure 3: Student evaluation scores demonstrating high ratings for clarity, organisation, and explanation — see evaluation reports in the evidence folder.]
crumbs: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
text:
Active Support and Interactive Engagement
Beyond clarity, I prioritise active support and engagement during learning. I adopt a highly interactive teaching style, regularly circulating during sessions, prompting discussion, and encouraging students to ask questions. I also integrate interactive tools such as Slido and Mentimeter into lectures, using live polls and questions to encourage participation, check understanding in real time, and engage students who may be less likely to contribute verbally. This approach supports a range of learning preferences and aligns with Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles by providing multiple means of engagement and participation.
crumbs: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
section: Separating Experimental and Analytical Components: Dedicated Dry Labs
text:
Separating Experimental and Analytical Components: Dedicated Dry Labs
A major development in my teaching practice has been the intentional separation of experimental and analytical components of learning. In BIOL340, I redesigned the practical sequence to introduce dedicated “dry labs” focused exclusively on data analysis and interpretation. This allows students to engage with experimental work and computational analysis as distinct cognitive tasks, reducing overload and improving conceptual understanding.
This approach is informed by cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988): separating these components minimises extraneous cognitive load and allows students to focus on the underlying concepts and skills. It also creates space to explicitly teach data analysis as a core scientific capability, rather than treating it as an implicit component of laboratory work.
crumbs: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
text:
Block-Based Learning Activities
In response to student feedback that aspects of coding were difficult to understand, I introduced a series of block-based learning activities designed to shift the focus from syntax to conceptual understanding. In these activities, code is transformed into a set of physical “puzzle pieces” that students must organise into the correct analytical workflow. This enables students to engage with the logical structure of code without the additional cognitive demand of generating it independently.
This approach aligns with both cognitive load theory (reducing unnecessary complexity) and UDL principles (incorporating visual and tactile modes of learning alongside traditional instruction).






Block-based coding activity — physical puzzle pieces.







Online interface with modular code blocks.







Students working with block activities in class.





Figure 4: Block-based coding activity and corresponding online modular code interface.
This approach is reinforced through a custom-built teaching website, where code is presented in modular “blocks” that students can copy and use directly in their analyses. Together, these strategies emphasise that the key learning outcome is understanding the purpose and function of each step, rather than memorising or reproducing code — supporting a diverse student cohort by accommodating different levels of prior experience and preferred learning styles.
crumbs: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
section: Inclusive and Psychologically Safe Learning Environments
text:
Inclusive and Psychologically Safe Learning Environments
I intentionally design and facilitate learning environments that promote inclusivity, psychological safety, and active student participation. I explicitly normalise uncertainty and difficulty as part of the learning process, framing challenges as expected and manageable. This is reflected in student feedback indicating increased confidence in engaging with computational and data-driven content.
Student evaluation scores for respect, approachability, and encouragement of participation are consistently high (up to 6.0/6.0 in some cohorts).
CHEM325 (2024)



Student evaluation scores — CHEM325 2024.


BIOL215 (2025)



Student evaluation scores — BIOL215 2025.


Figure 5: Student evaluation scores and qualitative feedback highlighting supportive and engaging teaching environment.
Selected student comments:

“Nick was always very friendly, nice, and approachable. He answered our questions with a great sense of humour, which made the classroom environment much more engaging.”
— BIOL215 student, 2025


“I really liked how down-to-earth Nik was; during practicals he would frequently walk around and ask everyone how we were going and if we had any questions, which was really good when we were stuck.”
— BIOL215 student, 2025
crumbs: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
text:
Collaborative and Structured Learning Formats
I also employ a range of structured and collaborative learning activities, including think–pair–share discussions and guided problem-solving tasks within practical sessions. These ensure that students can engage with content in multiple ways, supporting diverse learning preferences and prior knowledge levels.
The introduction of block-based thinking activities further supports inclusivity by translating abstract computational concepts into tangible, manipulable forms, allowing students to build understanding without being constrained by coding syntax — developed in direct response to student feedback indicating that traditional coding instruction was a barrier to engagement.






Evidence: Pre- and post-dry-practical surveys and teaching evaluation reports are available in the evidence folder.




Previous: Qualifier 1 — Curriculum Design · Next: Qualifier 3 — Learning Pathways
crumbs: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
text:
Qualifier 3 · Learning Pathways
I actively support students in navigating both formal and informal learning pathways by making learning processes explicit and providing structured opportunities for choice, progression, and self-directed development. A central principle of my curriculum design is that students should not only develop disciplinary knowledge, but should develop the ability to navigate their own learning — into the subject, through the degree, and into professional practice.
This is reflected in three interconnected dimensions: transition into the curriculum, navigation through the course, and preparation for the workforce.
crumbs: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
section: Transition Into and Through the Curriculum
text:
Transition Into and Through the Curriculum
Within BIOL340, transition into the curriculum is supported through:

Clearly scaffolded practical sequences
Curated learning resources (step-by-step guides, annotated code, and exemplar outputs)
Flexible entry points that allow students to engage with material at an appropriate level of complexity

Recognising that many students arrive with limited experience in computational biology, the early weeks of BIOL340 are designed explicitly to support the transition from the more familiar laboratory science of BIOL215 into data-intensive analytical work. I normalise this transition in my teaching language and task design, framing the challenge of learning new tools as an expected and manageable part of becoming a biologist.

Formal and Informal Pathways
Students are encouraged to move between formal learning environments (scheduled labs, workshops, and assessments) and informal pathways, such as independent practice, peer collaboration, and extension materials embedded within the subject’s Moodle site.
A key strategy is the integration of staged assessment and reflective tasks, which support students in understanding not only what they are learning, but how they are learning. The transition from a structured scientific report to a generative AI reflection task requires students to evaluate their own learning processes, tool use, and decision-making — promoting metacognitive awareness and helping students navigate increasingly independent learning contexts.
crumbs: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
text:
Preparation for the Workforce
A deliberate focus of my curriculum redesign in both BIOL340 and CHEM325 has been the explicit framing of computational and digital literacy as essential graduate skills for modern biologists. As biology becomes increasingly data-driven, graduates are expected to engage with real datasets, use analytical tools confidently, and communicate findings in ways that reflect contemporary scientific practice.
My curriculum redesign is premised on this reality: that preparing students for the workforce requires embedding the tools, workflows, and ways of thinking they will encounter in research and industry roles.
This framing is reflected in student feedback. In response to the personalised survey question “This subject has made me feel equipped to work in the field of Cell and Molecular Biology,” students in BIOL340 returned:

Mean score: 4.75/6.0
80% agreeing or strongly agreeing

Qualitative responses further reinforce this connection — students noted that computational activities were valuable because “it is what we will be using in the industry” and that the subject reflected “current techniques.”
crumbs: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
text:
Theoretical Underpinning
These approaches are informed by theories of student transition and self-regulated learning (Zimmerman, 2002), which emphasise the importance of scaffolding, feedback, and learner agency in developing independence.
Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles are embedded by offering multiple pathways for engagement and progression, ensuring that students with diverse backgrounds and prior experiences can successfully navigate the subject.
The custom-built teaching website I have developed provides modular code blocks, worked examples, and extension materials that students can access independently — bridging structured teaching and self-directed learning outside scheduled sessions.






Evidence: BIOL340 pre- and post-dry-practical surveys and student evaluation reports are available in the evidence folder. See also the BIOL340 teaching website.




Previous: Qualifier 2 — Inclusive Learning · Next: Qualifier 4 — Scaffolded Assessment
crumbs: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Qualifier 4 · Scaffolded Assessment
My approach to assessment is grounded in the principle that assessment should both evaluate learning and actively support it. I design assessment tasks that reflect authentic scientific practice, requiring students to analyse data, interpret results, and communicate findings clearly. In subjects such as BIOL340 and CHEM325/BIOL984, this involves moving beyond recall-based tasks toward assessments that mirror real-world biological workflows — including data analysis, visualisation, and scientific writing.
crumbs: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Constructive Alignment
Assessment design is guided by constructive alignment (Biggs & Tang, 2011), ensuring that tasks are directly linked to intended learning outcomes and supported by teaching activities. In BIOL340, this is exemplified through a staged assessment structure that aligns with the progression from experimental work to computational analysis and interpretation. Students are required to engage with authentic datasets and produce outputs that reflect real scientific practice, ensuring coherence between what is taught, what is practised, and what is assessed.
crumbs: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
The Multi-Stage Assessment Model
A key innovation in my assessment practice has been the development of a multi-stage assessment model, informed by my Learning and Teaching grant project and aligned with institutional priorities around authentic assessment and academic integrity.
In this model, students first complete a peer-reviewed scientific report, requiring them to analyse data and communicate findings in a formal scientific format. This is followed by a generative AI reflection task, where students critically evaluate their use of AI tools in the writing and analysis process.
This staged design serves several pedagogical purposes:

Separation of skill domains — Separating the development of scientific communication skills from reflective and evaluative thinking reduces cognitive overload and allows students to focus on distinct learning objectives at each stage.
AI literacy and ethical awareness — Explicitly embedding AI literacy and ethical awareness into the curriculum ensures that students are not only using emerging tools, but critically engaging with their limitations and implications.
Iterative feedback — Creating opportunities for iterative feedback allows students to reflect on and improve their work across stages, rather than receiving feedback only at the end of the process.
crumbs: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Alignment with UOW Frameworks
This approach aligns with the UOW Standards and Quality Framework (particularly its integration of design, delivery, and performance) and maps directly onto the UOW Assessment and Feedback Principles:







Principle
How It Is Addressed




Alignment
Constructive alignment of assessment with learning outcomes and teaching activities


Authenticity
Real datasets, peer review, and scientific report formats reflecting genuine research practice


Transparency
Explicit marking criteria, annotated exemplars, and structured task descriptors


Feedback
Formative checkpoints, in-class verbal feedback, and staged structure enabling action on feedback


Diversity & Inclusion
UDL-informed design reducing barriers related to prior coding experience while maintaining high expectations
crumbs: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Student Evaluation Data
Student evaluation data indicates strong performance in areas relating to clarity of assessment expectations, with mean scores typically above 5.5/6.0, suggesting that students understand what is required and how their work will be evaluated.



Student evaluation scores relating to clarity of assessment expectations.





Additional evaluation data for assessment design.


Figure 6: Student evaluation scores relating to clarity of assessment expectations.
crumbs: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Responding to Feedback
Evaluation data highlights feedback as an area for continued development, particularly in relation to timeliness and perceived usefulness (approximately 5.2–5.5/6.0). In response, I have implemented several changes:

Introducing formative checkpoints within practical sequences
Providing clear marking criteria and annotated exemplars
Increasing in-class, verbal feedback during practical sessions
Restructuring workflows to improve feedback turnaround time

These changes reflect a shift toward assessment for learning (Boud & Falchikov, 2006; Nicol & Macfarlane-Dick, 2006), where feedback is integrated throughout the learning process rather than occurring solely at the end.
crumbs: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
UDL and Cognitive Load
Assessment design is also informed by principles of Universal Design for Learning and cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988). Tasks are structured to prioritise conceptual understanding over technical reproduction, ensuring that students are assessed on their ability to interpret data, justify decisions, and communicate meaning — rather than on prior experience with coding or technical syntax. The staged nature of assessment further reduces cognitive overload by breaking complex tasks into manageable components, supporting a diverse cohort with varying levels of preparedness.






Evidence: Assessment design documents, marking rubrics, and the L&T grant application are available in the evidence folder.




Previous: Qualifier 3 — Learning Pathways · Next: Qualifier 5 — Scholarship of T&L
crumbs: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Qualifier 7 · Professional Practice
My professional practice is characterised by a systematic and responsive approach to course management, underpinned by a commitment to continuous improvement, effective organisation, and adaptability in the face of evolving disciplinary and institutional demands.
crumbs: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Subject Coordination and Course Management
As Subject Coordinator for BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology), I am responsible for the overall design, delivery, and administration of the subject, including:

Coordination of a multi-member teaching team
Management of practical sessions across wet and dry labs
Alignment of assessment with learning outcomes
Communication with students

The teaching team I coordinate comprises three academic staff with distinct disciplinary specialisations — Dr Jody Gorman, Professor Lezanne Ooi, and Dr Dzung Do-Ha — as well as a Technical Officer, Dr Mitchell St Claire-Glover, who supports practical session delivery. Managing this team requires clear communication, proactive organisation, and the creation of shared materials and frameworks that enable each member to contribute effectively while maintaining a coherent student experience.
I also teach into CHEM325/BIOL984 (Bioinformatics) and BIOL215, contributing to the coordination and delivery of subjects across both undergraduate and postgraduate levels.

Practical Demands of Coordination
BIOL340 involves a parallel sequence of wet and dry practical sessions, each requiring advance preparation of materials, briefing of teaching staff, and coordination of booking and equipment logistics with technical staff. I manage subject communication with students via Moodle announcements and email, handle queries relating to assessment and curriculum, and oversee academic administration including grade management and moderation.


Leading Curriculum Change
The substantial redesign of BIOL340 that I have led — transitioning from a quiz-only assessment model to a scaffolded, multi-stage structure incorporating wet labs, dry labs, peer review, scientific writing, and generative AI reflection — demonstrates my capacity to manage complex curriculum change while maintaining subject coherence and quality. This redesign required coordinating across multiple teaching and technical staff, developing new practical materials, and ensuring alignment with institutional standards and quality frameworks.
crumbs: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
section: Development of Teaching Resources and Infrastructure
text:
Development of Teaching Resources and Infrastructure
I have invested significantly in developing sustainable teaching resources that support both students and colleagues:

Custom-built teaching website for BIOL340, featuring modular code blocks, annotated workflows, and step-by-step guides that students can access independently
Quarto-based practical materials and annotated datasets designed to be reusable and adaptable across teaching sessions
Pre- and post-surveys to evaluate the impact of teaching innovations (such as the introduction of dry practicals), providing a structured mechanism for evidence-based reflection and continuous improvement

These resources reduce reliance on ad hoc instruction, improve consistency across teaching sessions, and support the scalability of teaching innovations — reflecting a professional approach to course management that prioritises sustainability and quality.
crumbs: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Resilience and Adaptability
A defining feature of my professional practice has been my capacity to respond constructively to significant changes in the higher education landscape, particularly the emergence of generative AI. Rather than viewing AI as a threat to assessment integrity alone, I have engaged proactively with the challenge:

Developing new assessment models that embed AI literacy
Contributing to school-wide policy through the SSCI AI Working Group
Sharing practical strategies with colleagues through workshops and presentations

This proactive, solutions-oriented approach reflects professional resilience and a commitment to turning disruption into opportunity for pedagogical improvement.
crumbs: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Balancing Teaching and Research
I also balance my teaching responsibilities with an active research program in molecular entomology and bioinformatics, maintaining a research laboratory group while contributing to teaching innovation. This dual engagement ensures that my teaching remains informed by current disciplinary practice and that my professional practice is grounded in both scholarly and practical expertise.
crumbs: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Summary
My professional practice is characterised by effective organisation, a commitment to evidence-based improvement, and the capacity to lead and manage change. Through sustained investment in teaching resources, collaborative engagement with colleagues, and proactive responses to emerging challenges, I aim to ensure that the subjects I coordinate are well-managed, pedagogically sound, and responsive to the needs of students and the discipline.






Evidence: Teaching coordination materials, teaching website, and pre/post survey instruments are available in the evidence folder.




Previous: Qualifier 6 — Evaluation & CPD · Back to: Home
crumbs: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Teaching Philosophy
My teaching is grounded in the view that digital and computational literacy is essential for modern biology graduates. As biology becomes increasingly data-driven, students must be able to interpret data, use computational tools effectively, and communicate findings clearly and ethically. These capabilities are central to developing critical thinking, problem solving, and communication skills.

title: Teaching Philosophy
section: Embedding Computational Skills in Disciplinary Contexts
text:
Embedding Computational Skills in Disciplinary Contexts
I approach the teaching of computational skills by embedding them within disciplinary contexts rather than presenting them as abstract techniques. This reflects an interdisciplinary perspective that integrates biology with data science and supports students in applying knowledge across domains. It also recognises that confidence with quantitative and computational skills develops through supported, meaningful engagement (Bandura, 1997; Prince, 2004).

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Active Participation and Authentic Learning
Active participation is a core principle of my teaching. I design learning experiences that mirror authentic scientific workflows, including data analysis, visualisation, interpretation, and writing. These are structured through constructive alignment, ensuring that learning outcomes, activities, and assessment are clearly connected (Biggs & Tang, 2011).

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Assessment and Feedback
Assessment is designed to be authentic and to require application, justification, and clear communication. I incorporate opportunities for feedback and reflection, supporting the development of self-regulated learners. Where appropriate, I integrate generative AI in a structured way, requiring students to evaluate and refine outputs. This supports ethical engagement with emerging technologies and aligns with contemporary approaches to assessment and feedback (Boud & Falchikov, 2006; Nicol & Macfarlane-Dick, 2006).

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Inclusive Learning Environments
I aim to create inclusive learning environments in which students feel supported to engage with challenging material and develop confidence in their abilities. This includes clear expectations, scaffolded learning, and opportunities for practice and feedback.

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Summary
Overall, my teaching focuses on supporting students to become capable and adaptable graduates. By aligning learning with authentic disciplinary practice and providing structured support, I aim to enable deep engagement with biology while developing transferable skills for future study and professional practice.







Next: Explore how these principles are enacted across the seven CPD qualifiers.

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Qualifier 6 · Evaluation & CPD
I actively use evaluation data to reflect on and improve my teaching practice, and I contribute to the professional development of teaching teams within my school. My approach to evaluation is informed by a commitment to evidence-based reflection, where student feedback, peer input, and personal experience collectively shape iterative improvements to my teaching.
crumbs: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Engaging with Student Evaluation Data
I regularly review and reflect on Teaching Evaluation (TEBS) data across the subjects I teach.

CHEM325/BIOL984 (Bioinformatics)
Combined evaluation data has consistently demonstrated strong performance, with an overall mean of 5.45/6.0.
I engage critically with areas where evaluation data identifies opportunities for improvement. For example, feedback relating to timeliness of feedback and clarity of assessment expectations (approximately 5.17–5.27/6.0 in BIOL340) has informed specific changes to my practice, including the introduction of formative checkpoints, clearer marking criteria, and restructured feedback workflows.
Qualitative feedback from students has also shaped my teaching: students have noted the value of interactive elements such as Slido and live polling, the clarity of structured explanations, and the supportive learning environment, while also identifying areas where communication about assessment requirements could be strengthened. I use this feedback as a direct input into subject redesign and teaching refinement.
[Figure 8: Longitudinal evaluation data showing trends across BIOL340 and CHEM325/BIOL984 — see evaluation reports in the evidence folder.]
crumbs: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Supporting and Mentoring Teaching Teams
As Subject Coordinator for BIOL340, I am responsible for coordinating and supporting a teaching team that includes academic staff with diverse specialisations and a technical officer. Managing this team requires deliberate coordination, clear communication, and the creation of shared resources and frameworks that allow each member to contribute effectively to a coherent student experience.
I support team coherence through several mechanisms:

Pre-lab orientation materials — Prior to each practical session, I prepare and share detailed materials that set out the learning objectives, expected student background, and key technical and pedagogical points. These ensure all teaching staff can orient students consistently and address questions with confidence.
Pre-session briefings — I hold briefings with the teaching team to discuss session goals, anticipated student challenges, and any adjustments to materials based on feedback from previous cohorts.
Peer mentoring — I work alongside colleagues to share pedagogical strategies — for example, discussing how the scaffolded practical structure supports students with limited computational experience, or how the block-based coding activities can be facilitated to maximise engagement.
crumbs: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
section: Supporting Colleagues Through the SSCI AI Working Group
text:
Supporting Colleagues Through the SSCI AI Working Group
I contribute to supporting colleagues through my active role in the SSCI AI Working Group, where I have helped establish school-wide guidelines for teaching with AI and designing resilient assessments. This work has involved leading workshops and delivering presentations to academic staff across the School of Science, sharing both conceptual frameworks and practical tools developed through my scholarship. Specific contributions include:

Presenting the GARI (Generative AI Risk Index) framework to school colleagues, providing a shared language and structured approach for evaluating and mitigating AI-related risks in assessment design.
Co-developing guidelines for the ethical and pedagogically sound integration of generative AI into teaching and assessment across the school.
Delivering workshops on AI in research and teaching contexts, supporting colleagues in developing their own approaches to AI-responsive pedagogy.


Concrete Impact: GARI Pilot Consultation
The practical impact of this mentoring is illustrated by one concrete example from the GARI pilot. Following a consultation arising from the working group’s assessment review, one subject coordinator reflected on a high-risk assessment and recognised that two of the GARI framework’s built-in mitigation strategies were applicable to their context: reducing the assessment’s weighting (thereby reducing the incentive for AI misuse) and providing explicit, specific guidance to students about permissible AI use.
By implementing these two targeted adjustments, the coordinator was able to reduce the assessment’s composite risk classification from “high” to “moderate” — without requiring a full redesign. This outcome illustrates how the GARI framework functions not only as an analytical tool, but as a practical scaffold that supports educators in taking evidence-based, proportionate action.






Evidence: SSCI AI Working Group presentation materials, TEBS evaluation reports, and CPD records are available in the evidence folder.
crumbs: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Engaging in Continuing Professional Development
My engagement in CPD is driven by a recognition that effective teaching requires ongoing learning and adaptation. I have pursued professional development through multiple pathways:

Engagement with teaching and learning scholarship (detailed in Qualifier 5)
Attendance at institutional and external conferences
Collaborative work with colleagues on curriculum and assessment innovation
Sustained involvement in the SSCI AI Working Group

I view this portfolio as itself an exercise in reflective professional development — an opportunity to critically evaluate my teaching in relation to the CPD framework and identify areas for ongoing growth. Feedback from students, evaluation data, and the experience of scholarly engagement continue to shape my development as an educator.

Previous: Qualifier 5 — Scholarship of T&L · Next: Qualifier 7 — Professional Practice
crumbs: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
text:
Qualifier 5 · Scholarship of T&L
My engagement with the scholarship of teaching and learning is focused on the intersection of assessment design, generative AI in higher education, and computational skills development in biology. I have pursued this scholarship through funded projects, conference presentations, collaborative research, and the development of practical frameworks that contribute to both institutional practice and the broader higher education sector.
crumbs: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
section: Learning and Teaching Grant: Redesigning Assessment in BIOL340
text:
Learning and Teaching Grant: Redesigning Assessment in BIOL340
I was awarded a UOW Learning and Teaching grant to support the redesign of assessment in BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology). This project focused on transitioning the subject from a quiz-only assessment structure to an authentic, multi-stage model that integrates scientific report writing, peer review, and structured engagement with generative AI. The grant supported the development and evaluation of this approach, which has since become a central feature of the subject and informed my broader scholarship on AI-responsive assessment design.
crumbs: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
section: The Generative AI Risk Index (GARI) Framework
text:
The Generative AI Risk Index (GARI) Framework
In collaboration with the School of Science (SSCI) AI Working Group, I co-developed a four-dimensional risk assessment framework for evaluating generative AI-related risks in higher education assessment.
The GARI framework conceptualises assessment risk as the balance between:

Exposure dimensions — task susceptibility and delivery mode
Mitigation dimensions — authenticity/personalisation and guidance/education

This work emerged from a systematic, school-wide analysis of all assessments conducted through the SSCI AI Working Group, involving both subject outline review and subject coordinator interviews. The framework was piloted across 2025 assessments within the School of Science, producing composite risk profiles that enable educators to identify vulnerabilities and target redesign efforts.
A scholarly paper is currently in preparation for submission to a peer-reviewed journal, framing the work within assessment validity theory, constructive alignment, and risk-based assurance approaches. The paper positions the GARI framework as a practical, educator-facing tool that addresses a significant gap in the literature — the absence of structured, operational tools for evaluating GenAI-related assessment risks in relation to learning outcome assurance.
I have also presented this framework at the university level through:

SSCI L&T planning days — to guide assessment redesign at the school level
SMAH faculty L&T forum
Sydney Basin STEM Education and Research Practice Symposium 2026 — as a workshop on Generative AI and assessment design, presented with collaborator Dr Jody Gorman
crumbs: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
text:
Conference Presentations and Dissemination
My teaching scholarship has been presented at multiple forums:

SMAH Learning and Teaching Forum

2024: Presented the multi-stage assessment model developed in BIOL340 and plans on how generative AI tools would be utilised to improve how students learn coding tools and self-assess writing.
2025: Presented “A Dimensional Risk Assessment of Generative AI in School of Science Assessments: A Framework for Transparent Evaluation”, sharing the GARI framework and its application.



Early Career Research Conference, UOW 2025
Delivered a workshop on “How to Utilise Generative AI to Enhance Scientific Writing Education”, sharing practical applications of AI to improve tertiary science education workflows with colleagues across disciplines.


Australian Conference on Science and Mathematics Education (ACSME) 2025
This presentation argued that rather than treating generative AI as a threat to be avoided, it should be embedded as a teachable graduate skill. The core thesis was that the modern cell biology graduate requires not only practical skills, data analysis, and core content knowledge, but also competence in using GenAI ethically — and that assessment should model and assure this.
The presentation reported on the redesign of BIOL340 assessment and its outcomes for student learning, AI literacy, and assessment integrity, framing GenAI in assessment as a “wicked problem” requiring nuanced, pedagogically sound responses.


Sydney Basin STEM Education and Research Practice Symposium 2026
Presented a workshop on assuring assessment integrity in the generative AI era, showcasing the Generative AI Risk Index (GARI) framework.
crumbs: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
text:
Summary
These presentations demonstrate my commitment to disseminating teaching innovations and contributing to scholarly conversations about teaching and learning in the context of generative AI. The progression from institutional grants through to conference presentation and manuscript preparation reflects an intentional trajectory toward sustained engagement with the scholarship of teaching and learning.






Evidence: Conference presentation slides, L&T grant documentation, and the GARI framework paper scaffold are available in the evidence folder.




Previous: Qualifier 4 — Scaffolded Assessment · Next: Qualifier 6 — Evaluation & CPD
crumbs: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree
text:
Qualifier 1 · Curriculum Design
My approach to the design and planning of learning activities is grounded in the principle that modern biology education must develop not only conceptual understanding, but also the ability to work with data, interpret results, and communicate findings effectively. As Subject Coordinator for BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology), I have led a substantial redesign of the subject to align learning activities with authentic scientific practice, ensuring that students engage with the kinds of workflows they will encounter beyond university.
crumbs: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree
section: Integration Across the Degree: A Scaffolded Skills Progression
text:
Integration Across the Degree: A Scaffolded Skills Progression
My curriculum design decisions span three interconnected subjects that students typically encounter in sequence across their degree.

In BIOL215, students are introduced to foundational skills in genetics, genomics, and the analysis of sequence data. This provides the disciplinary and conceptual grounding for more advanced computational work.
In BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology, Autumn), students build on this foundation through data analysis, visualisation, and interpretation using R, with computational skills now embedded within an experimental biology context.
In CHEM325/BIOL984 (Bioinformatics, Spring), the majority of the subject is dedicated to applying and extending these skills across a range of bioinformatics workflows — the most advanced treatment of computational biology in the undergraduate and postgraduate sequence.

This progression represents an intentional, degree-level design decision: rather than treating computational skills as isolated, subject-specific content, I have worked to ensure that each subject builds on and reinforces the skills developed in the previous one. Students arrive in BIOL340 with sequence analysis experience from BIOL215; they arrive in CHEM325/BIOL984 with data analysis experience from BIOL340. The result is a curriculum in which computational literacy develops progressively across the degree, culminating in advanced bioinformatics practice.
This alignment is consistent with the UOW Standards and Quality Framework for Learning, Teaching and Research Training, which emphasises that courses should be structured to provide coherence and progressive development of student capabilities.
crumbs: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree
text:
Subject-Level Design: BIOL340
Central to this redesign is the concept of constructive alignment (Biggs & Tang, 2011), where learning outcomes, teaching activities, and assessment are intentionally integrated. In BIOL340, this is achieved through a scaffolded sequence of wet and dry practicals that mirror a complete research pipeline. Students begin with experimental data generation (e.g., cell viability assays), and then transition into data analysis, visualisation, and interpretation using R. This structure ensures that computational skills are not taught in isolation, but are embedded within meaningful biological contexts.



Overview of BIOL340 practical sequence showing alignment between wet lab, dry lab, and assessment tasks.





Assessment alignment diagram for BIOL340.


Figure 1: Overview of BIOL340 practical sequence showing alignment between wet lab, dry lab, and assessment tasks.

Scaffolding for Diverse Student Backgrounds
A key design consideration has been scaffolding for diverse student backgrounds, particularly in computational skills. Recognising that many students enter with limited experience in coding or quantitative analysis, I have structured learning activities to progressively build competence. Early tasks provide highly guided, step-by-step instruction, while later activities require increasing independence in data interpretation and problem-solving. This approach is informed by cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988), reducing extraneous load while maintaining appropriate academic challenge.
To support this, I have developed a suite of custom teaching materials, including a beginner-friendly website, practicals, annotated datasets, and structured workflows that explicitly guide students through the process of importing, cleaning, analysing, and visualising data. These materials are designed to be both instructional and reusable, allowing students to revisit concepts as needed.



Example of Quarto practical showing scaffolded data analysis workflow.


Figure 2: Example of Quarto practical showing scaffolded data analysis workflow. View dry practical 1 website
crumbs: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree
text:
Integration into CHEM325/BIOL984
This design philosophy extends beyond BIOL340 into my teaching in bioinformatics (CHEM325/BIOL984), where I similarly emphasise the integration of theory with applied, real-world datasets. I have redesigned assessments in CHEM325 to equip students with the skills to analyse large-scale real-world datasets, including a formal report that explores the evolution and epidemiology of Zika virus. I similarly apply principles of constructive alignment where computer practicals allow students to obtain and analyse their data, which then directly translates into the results section of their formal report.
Student feedback consistently highlights the value of this approach, with mean scores of approximately 5.8/6.0 for connecting theory with practical applications — indicating that students recognise the relevance and coherence of the learning activities.
crumbs: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree
text:
Iterative Improvement
Importantly, the design of learning activities is informed by ongoing reflection and evaluation. Feedback from BIOL340 identified that while students valued the authenticity of computational tasks, some experienced difficulty when transitioning to independent analysis. In response, I have refined activity design to include clearer transitions between guided and independent work, additional explanatory material, and more explicit links between each analytical step and its biological meaning. This iterative approach reflects a commitment to continuous improvement aligned with the UOW Standards and Quality Framework for Learning, Teaching and Research Training.






Evidence: Pre- and post-dry-practical survey data are available in the evidence folder. See also the BIOL340 dry practical website.




Next: Qualifier 2 — Inclusive Learning Experiences
crumbs: Qualifier 1: Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree

title: CPD Level 2 ePortfolio
text:
Lecturer in Bioinformatics
    School of Chemistry and Molecular Bioscience · Faculty of Science, Medicine and Health · University of Wollongong
    CPD Level 2 Portfolio · 2025

title: CPD Level 2 ePortfolio
text:
Welcome
This ePortfolio documents my continuing professional development in learning and teaching at the University of Wollongong. It presents evidence of my practice across the seven CPD Level 2 qualifiers, grounded in my disciplinary context at the interface of biological research and computational science.
Use the navigation above to explore each section, or browse the qualifiers below.

title: CPD Level 2 ePortfolio
text:
Statement of Context
I am a Lecturer in Bioinformatics in the School of Chemistry and Molecular Bioscience (SCMB), Faculty of Science, Medicine and Health (SMAH), at the University of Wollongong. My teaching is shaped by my disciplinary background at the interface of biological research and computational science. My work in data-intensive biology — including genomics, molecular entomology, and bioinformatics — has centred on analysing and interpreting complex datasets. This experience informs my understanding of what constitutes meaningful learning in contemporary biology, where data-intensive approaches, computational thinking, and digital tools are integral to both research and industry practice.
The context in which I teach is defined by a shift toward data-driven biology, where computational approaches underpin research across subdisciplines. Graduates are expected to engage critically with data, apply analytical tools appropriately, and communicate findings clearly. At the same time, many students enter biology with limited experience or confidence in quantitative and computational skills, creating a need for structured, supportive learning environments that enable skill development while maintaining disciplinary rigour.
In response, my teaching integrates opportunities for students to work with real biological data, develop analytical skills, and engage with tools used in research and industry. Learning activities reflect authentic scientific practices and are embedded within the curriculum. I am Subject Coordinator for BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology), and teach into CHEM325/BIOL984 (Bioinformatics: Genome, Genes and Biomolecules / Applied Bioinformatics) and BIOL215. More recently, the emergence of generative AI has further shaped this context, requiring approaches that support transparent, critical, and appropriate use of these technologies. My practice continues to evolve through reflection, feedback, and engagement with ongoing developments in both biology and higher education.

title: CPD Level 2 ePortfolio
text:
CPD Qualifiers


  
    Qualifier 1
    Effectively Design Curriculum Demonstrating Integration Across the Degree
    Scaffolded skills progression across BIOL215, BIOL340, and CHEM325/BIOL984; constructive alignment and authentic learning design.
  

  
    Qualifier 2
    Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences
    Interactive teaching strategies, block-based coding activities, dedicated dry labs, and UDL-informed practice.
  

  
    Qualifier 3
    Support Students in Choice and Navigation of Learning Pathways
    Transition support, self-directed learning, metacognitive awareness, and workforce preparation.
  

  
    Qualifier 4
    Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
    Multi-stage assessment model, peer review, AI reflection tasks, and evidence-based feedback strategies.
  

  
    Qualifier 5
    Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
    L&T grant, GARI framework development, and conference presentations on GenAI and assessment.
  

  
    Qualifier 6
    Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in CPD
    Critical engagement with TEBS data, teaching team coordination, and the SSCI AI Working Group.
  

  
    Qualifier 7
    Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
    Subject coordination, teaching infrastructure development, and adaptability in the generative AI era.
